content.text: Why Root Credentials Must Be Locked Away

The AWS root user has absolute, unrestricted authority over every resource, setting, and identity in an AWS account. Unlike an IAM administrator, the root user cannot be denied by Service Control Policies, permission boundaries, or any IAM construct. A compromised root credential means total account ownership — every resource deleted, every secret exfiltrated, every other identity revoked.

Locking away root credentials is not a security suggestion — it is a baseline security requirement. Every major compliance framework (CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark, SOC 2, PCI DSS, NIST 800-53) explicitly requires it. The goal is to make root access so inconvenient and monitored that it is used only for the handful of tasks that genuinely require it, typically fewer than five times per year.

The mental model: root is a physical master key. The safest master key is one sitting in a tamper-evident safe that requires two people to open, has a usage log, and triggers an alarm every time the safe door opens.

attrs.alt: Root credential security architecture showing four protection layers: no access keys, MFA required, strong password, CloudTrail monitoring, plus SCP layer for member accounts

content.text: Step 1: Delete Root Access Keys

Root access keys allow programmatic access to the AWS API with the same unlimited authority as the root user. AWS created root keys as a bootstrapping mechanism in the early days of the platform; they are now considered an unacceptable security risk for any account that has moved past initial setup.

If root access keys exist in your account, delete them immediately. There is no legitimate ongoing use case for them — every task that tempts you to use root keys can and should be done with an IAM role.
Checking for and Deleting Root Access Keys

# Check if root access keys exist (run as any IAM user with iam:GetAccountSummary)
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent'
# Returns 1 if root keys exist, 0 if none

# To delete root access keys, you must sign in to the console as root:
# 1. Sign in at https://console.aws.amazon.com with root email + password + MFA
# 2. Navigate to: account menu (top right) → Security credentials
# 3. Under "Access keys", find any existing keys
# 4. Choose Delete for each key

# Verify deletion via CLI (should return 0)
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent'

AWS Trusted Advisor and Security Hub both flag the presence of root access keys as a critical finding. If you inherit an account with root keys, treat their existence as a potential incident — check CloudTrail to see if and when they were used before deleting them.
Auditing Root Key Usage in CloudTrail Before Deletion

Before deleting root keys that someone else created, check their recent usage. If they were used recently, investigate why before deleting them — there may be automation that depends on them and will break.

# Look for API calls made with root access keys (last 90 days)
aws cloudtrail lookup-events     --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root     --start-time $(date -d '90 days ago' --iso-8601=seconds)     --query 'Events[*].{Time:EventTime,Event:EventName,Source:EventSource,IP:CloudTrailEvent}'     --output table

# Specifically look for non-console root events (programmatic root key usage)
# Console sign-ins appear as eventName=ConsoleLogin
# API calls via access keys appear as any other eventName with userIdentity.type=Root
Step 2: Enable MFA on the Root User

After deleting access keys, enabling MFA is the single most important security control for the root user. MFA means an attacker who steals the root password still cannot log in without the second factor — the physical token or authenticator app.
Choosing an MFA Type
MFA Type
Device
Strength
Best For
Hardware FIDO2 / WebAuthn
YubiKey, Titan key
Highest — phishing-resistant
Production accounts, organizations
Hardware TOTP token
Gemalto device
High — physical possession required
Air-gapped environments
Virtual MFA (TOTP app)
Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password
Good — software-based
Development accounts, small teams
Passkey (preview)
Platform authenticator
High — phishing-resistant
Accounts supporting passkeys

For production and organization management accounts, use a hardware security key. Virtual MFA apps on mobile devices create a dependency on a personal phone that may be lost, stolen, or have the app removed. Hardware tokens can be stored in a physical vault alongside the root password.
Setting Up MFA on the Root User
Sign in to the AWS console as root (email + password).
Navigate to account menu (top right) -> Security credentials.
Under Multi-factor authentication (MFA), choose Assign MFA device.
Select the MFA type (Authenticator app for virtual TOTP, or Security key for FIDO2/hardware).
Follow the wizard: scan the QR code with your authenticator app or register the hardware key.
Enter two consecutive TOTP codes (to confirm the device is synced) and choose Add MFA.
Test immediately: sign out and sign back in — you should be prompted for the MFA code.
Storing the MFA Device Alongside Root Credentials
The MFA device and the root password belong together in controlled, documented storage. Recommended approach:
Password manager vault entry

content: : Store the root email, root password, and virtual MFA backup codes in a password manager vault accessible to ≥2 authorized people. Some password managers (1Password, Bitwarden Business) support storing TOTP secrets directly.

content.text: Hardware key physical vault

content: : Keep hardware security keys in a tamper-evident physical envelope or small lockbox. Label the key with the account ID and keep it with the account runbook.

content.text: MFA backup codes

content: : When you register virtual MFA, AWS provides backup codes. Print them, put them in a sealed envelope, and store in the same vault. These allow recovery if the MFA device is lost.

content.text: Two-person rule

content: : No single person should be able to retrieve and use root credentials alone. Split access between two authorized personnel, or require two people to witness root logins.

content.text: Step 3: Use a Strong, Unique Root Password

The root password should be long, random, and unique — never reused across any other account or service. Since you will use root fewer than five times per year, there is no need to memorize it. Generate it randomly and store it securely.

# Generate a strong random password using openssl (local, no AWS calls needed)
openssl rand -base64 32
# Example output: K8mN2vQxP4rL9jF1hT6wZ3nB7cY0uA5d+E=

# Or using Python (available on any system)
python3 -c "import secrets, string; chars = string.ascii_letters + string.digits + '!@#%^&*-_'; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))"

# After generating: store it in your password manager before using it
Password requirements from a security perspective:
Minimum 32 characters — longer passwords are exponentially harder to crack
Mix of uppercase, lowercase, digits, and symbols
Never based on words, names, dates, or any pattern
Never reused from any other account — especially no other AWS account
Rotating the Root Password Annually

Even for rarely-used credentials, annual rotation limits the window of exposure if the password was ever observed, logged, or leaked. To rotate: sign in as root, navigate to Security credentials, choose Password, and update it. Store the new password in your vault before logging out.
Step 4: Store Root Credentials in a Secrets Vault
"Locking away" root credentials means placing them in a secrets management system with access controls, audit logs, and access restrictions. Appropriate vault options vary by organization size:
Vault Option
Best For
Audit Trail
Notes
Enterprise password manager (1Password Business, Bitwarden Teams)
Teams of 2-50 people
Yes — per-user access logs
Supports TOTP storage, vault sharing, access controls
HashiCorp Vault
DevOps-heavy teams
Yes — detailed audit log
Can enforce approval workflows before revealing secrets
AWS Secrets Manager
AWS-native workflows
Yes — CloudTrail integration
Ironic but valid: store root creds in Secrets Manager under an IAM-restricted path
Physical safe + printed runbook
Air-gapped / regulated
Manual log required
For accounts where digital systems can't be fully trusted

Whichever vault you use, document the retrieval process. The runbook entry for root credentials should include: which vault, which entry name, who has access, when credentials were last rotated, and the step-by-step procedure for using root (including what to do after the task is complete).
Step 5: Monitor Root Activity with CloudTrail
After locking away root credentials, monitoring is the final layer. Any root console login should trigger an immediate security alert. Root activity that you did not plan is a potential compromise.
Creating a Root Login Alert

# This assumes CloudTrail is already logging to CloudWatch Logs.
# If not, first create a trail and point it to a CloudWatch Logs group.

# 1. Create a metric filter that counts root console sign-ins
aws logs put-metric-filter     --log-group-name "aws-cloudtrail-logs"     --filter-name "RootConsoleLogin"     --filter-pattern '{ $.userIdentity.type = "Root" && $.eventType = "AwsConsoleSignIn" }'     --metric-transformations         metricName=RootConsoleLoginCount,metricNamespace=Security,metricValue=1

# 2. Create an SNS topic for security alerts
ROOT_ALERT_ARN=$(aws sns create-topic --name root-login-alerts --query TopicArn --output text)

# 3. Subscribe your security team
aws sns subscribe     --topic-arn "${ROOT_ALERT_ARN}"     --protocol email     --notification-endpoint security@example.com

# 4. Create a CloudWatch alarm (triggers on any single root login)
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm     --alarm-name "RootConsoleLogin"     --alarm-description "Root user signed into the console"     --metric-name RootConsoleLoginCount     --namespace Security     --statistic Sum     --period 60     --threshold 1     --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold     --evaluation-periods 1     --alarm-actions "${ROOT_ALERT_ARN}"     --treat-missing-data notBreaching

echo "Root login alerting configured. SNS topic: ${ROOT_ALERT_ARN}"
Reviewing Root Activity Periodically

# Pull all root events from the past 30 days for a monthly security review
aws cloudtrail lookup-events     --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root     --start-time $(date -d '30 days ago' --iso-8601=seconds)     --query 'Events[*].{Time:EventTime,Event:EventName,IP:CloudTrailEvent}'     --output json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
events = json.load(sys.stdin)
if not events:
    print('No root activity in the past 30 days — good.')
else:
    print(f'WARNING: {len(events)} root event(s) found:')
    for e in events:
        print(f'  {e["Time"]} | {e["Event"]}')
"
Enforcing Root Lockout via AWS Organizations SCP

For member accounts in an AWS Organization, you can add an extra layer: a Service Control Policy that denies all actions by the root user. Even if an attacker somehow obtains a member account's root credentials, the SCP prevents any root action from succeeding within that account.
Apply this SCP to all OUs under your root OU that should never need root access day-to-day (which is virtually all of them). Exceptions:
Management account

content: : SCPs do not apply to the management account's own root user — you cannot SCP-block the management account root.

content.text: Break-glass OU

content: : If you need to perform a root-only action in a member account (rare), temporarily move it to an unprotected OU, perform the action, then move it back.

content.text: New accounts

content: : Apply the SCP immediately after account creation via Control Tower or your vending mechanism.

content.text: Verifying the SCP Applies to an Account

# Check which SCPs apply to an account (from the management account)
ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"

# Get all policies attached to the account's parent OU
aws organizations list-parents --child-id "${ACCOUNT_ID}"     --query 'Parents[0].Id' --output text | xargs -I{}     aws organizations list-policies-for-target --target-id {} --filter SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY     --query 'Policies[*].{Name:Name,Id:Id}' --output table

# Verify the deny-root SCP is in the list
The Root Credentials Runbook Template
Every account should have a documented runbook describing exactly how to access root credentials. This prevents ad-hoc, insecure retrieval under pressure. A minimal runbook entry:

Account Root Credentials Runbook
==================================
Account ID:     123456789012
Account Alias:  my-company-prod
Root Email:     aws-root@company.com  (shared mailbox — 3 people have access)

Password Location:
  1Password vault: "AWS Production Root"
  Vault access: alice@company.com, bob@company.com (requires both to approve)

MFA Device:
  Type: YubiKey 5C NFC
  Location: IT Security safe, drawer 3, labeled "AWS Prod Root Key"
  Backup TOTP: Printed sheet in same safe, envelope marked "AWS Prod MFA Backup"

Retrieval Procedure:
  1. Create an incident ticket explaining why root access is needed
  2. Get manager approval (minimum VP-level for production account)
  3. Retrieve password from 1Password (requires both alice + bob)
  4. Retrieve YubiKey from IT Security safe (requires two witnesses)
  5. Log in at console.aws.amazon.com -> root sign-in
  6. Perform ONLY the documented task
  7. Sign out immediately after task completion
  8. Return YubiKey to safe; re-seal envelope
  9. Close incident ticket with what action was performed and why

Alert Response:
  If you receive a root login alert and did NOT perform a root login,
  treat it as an active security incident. Immediately:
  - Alert the security team (security@company.com)
  - Check CloudTrail for actions taken during the session
  - Engage AWS Support if account compromise is suspected

Last Password Rotation: 2025-01-15
Last MFA Device Test:   2025-01-15
Next Scheduled Review:  2026-01-15
Verifying Your Root Lockdown with AWS Security Hub

AWS Security Hub runs continuous compliance checks against your account, including checks specifically for root user security. After implementing root lockdown, use Security Hub findings to verify your controls are in place:

# Enable Security Hub (if not already enabled)
aws securityhub enable-security-hub --enable-default-standards

# List findings related to root user (after Security Hub has run its first checks)
aws securityhub get-findings     --filters '{
        "Title": [{"Value": "root", "Comparison": "CONTAINS"}],
        "RecordState": [{"Value": "ACTIVE", "Comparison": "EQUALS"}],
        "WorkflowStatus": [{"Value": "NEW", "Comparison": "EQUALS"}]
    }'     --query 'Findings[*].{Title:Title,Severity:Severity.Label,Status:WorkflowState}'     --output table

# Key checks Security Hub runs for root:
# - [IAM.1] IAM policies should not allow full "*:*" administrative privileges
# - [IAM.4] IAM root user access key should not exist
# - [IAM.9] Virtual MFA should be enabled for the root user
# - [CloudTrail.1] CloudTrail should be enabled and configured with log file validation
CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark controls directly relevant to root lockdown:
Control ID
Description
How to Pass
CIS 1.1
Avoid the use of root account
No root logins in the past 90 days
CIS 1.5
Ensure MFA is enabled for the root user
MFA device registered and active
CIS 1.6
Ensure hardware MFA is enabled for the root user
Hardware FIDO2 or TOTP device (not virtual)
CIS 1.4
Ensure no root user access key exists
IAM account summary shows 0 root keys
Automating Root Lockdown Checks Across Multiple Accounts

In multi-account environments, manually verifying root lockdown for every account is not feasible. The solution is automated compliance checks that run periodically and surface any account that is out of compliance.
Using AWS Config to Detect Root Key Presence
AWS Config provides managed rules that continuously evaluate account configuration. The rule
root-account-mfa-enabled
checks whether MFA is active on root, and Security Hub's CIS Benchmark checks cover root access keys. You can also write a custom Config rule using Lambda:
Aggregating Findings Across an Organization

# From the management account, get IAM credential report for all accounts
# First, list all accounts in the organization
aws organizations list-accounts --query 'Accounts[*].Id' --output text | tr '	' '
' > /tmp/account_ids.txt

# For each account, assume an audit role and check root key status
while read ACCOUNT_ID; do
    # Assume the audit role (must pre-exist in all member accounts)
    CREDS=$(aws sts assume-role         --role-arn "arn:aws:iam::${ACCOUNT_ID}:role/SecurityAuditRole"         --role-session-name "RootCheck"         --query 'Credentials.[AccessKeyId,SecretAccessKey,SessionToken]'         --output text)
    
    export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$(echo $CREDS | awk '{print $1}')
    export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(echo $CREDS | awk '{print $2}')
    export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=$(echo $CREDS | awk '{print $3}')
    
    ROOT_KEYS=$(aws iam get-account-summary         --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent' --output text)
    ROOT_MFA=$(aws iam get-account-summary         --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled' --output text)
    
    if [ "$ROOT_KEYS" -gt "0" ] || [ "$ROOT_MFA" -eq "0" ]; then
        echo "NON-COMPLIANT: ${ACCOUNT_ID} — Keys:${ROOT_KEYS} MFA:${ROOT_MFA}"
    else
        echo "OK: ${ACCOUNT_ID}"
    fi
    
    unset AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY AWS_SESSION_TOKEN
done < /tmp/account_ids.txt
The SecurityAuditRole in each member account must have iam:GetAccountSummary permission and a trust policy allowing the management account (or a dedicated security/audit account) to assume it.
Root Credential Recovery Scenarios

Despite best practices, situations arise where root access is needed but the credentials are unavailable. Understanding the recovery options prevents a locked-out situation from becoming a permanent account loss.
Lost Root Password (MFA Device Still Available)
Go to the AWS sign-in page and choose Forgot password.
AWS sends a password reset link to the root email address.
Access the root mailbox, click the link, and set a new password.
Sign in with the new password and the MFA device.
Store the new password in your vault before closing the browser.
Lost MFA Device (Root Password Still Available)
Sign in with root email and password. AWS prompts for MFA.
Choose Troubleshoot MFA or Get another way to authenticate.
AWS offers to verify identity via the root email address or a registered phone number.
Follow the verification steps — this is why the root email must be a working mailbox.
Once past MFA, remove the lost device and register a new one immediately.
Lost Both Password and MFA Device
Contact AWS Support via a support case opened by a different IAM user (if one exists) or via phone.
AWS Support requires verification of account ownership: the root email address, the billing contact phone number, and potentially the last four digits of the payment method on file.
This process can take several business days. There is no shortcut.
This scenario is why the root email must be a monitored, permanent mailbox and the billing contact information must be current.
Prevention

is far easier than recovery. Test root login access (including MFA) at least annually during a planned maintenance window. Discovering a broken MFA registration during a drill costs nothing; discovering it during an emergency is extremely costly.

# Annual root credential test procedure (document in your runbook)
# 1. Retrieve credentials from vault (follow two-person rule)
# 2. Sign in at: https://console.aws.amazon.com (root sign-in option)
# 3. Verify MFA prompt appears and TOTP code works
# 4. Verify you can access: IAM, Billing, Account Settings pages
# 5. Sign out immediately — do not perform any changes during a test
# 6. Record the test date in your runbook:
echo "Root credential test completed: $(date)" >> /path/to/runbook.txt
# 7. Return all credentials to vault
Common Mistakes When Locking Away Root Credentials
These errors appear repeatedly across organizations that think they have secured root but have gaps:
Mistake
Risk
Fix
Root email is a personal address
The person leaves and the mailbox dies — account is permanently unrecoverable
Use a shared mailbox monitored by a team
Virtual MFA on a personal phone
Phone lost/stolen/factory-reset = MFA inaccessible
Use hardware MFA or store TOTP secret in team password manager
Root password in plaintext in a shared doc or wiki
Document access = account compromise
Store in a password manager vault with access controls and audit logs
Never tested root login after setup
MFA may have drifted out of sync; password may have been changed in another context
Test annually as a documented drill
Single person holds all root access
Key person risk: illness, departure, or compromise loses root access
Two-person rule: split credential access between ≥2 authorized people
Root access keys created for 'just this one task'
Keys remain active indefinitely; often forgotten
Delete access keys immediately after the task; prefer IAM roles
SCP blocks root but break-glass process is undocumented
When a root-only task genuinely arises, no one knows how to temporarily lift the SCP
Document the OU migration procedure in the runbook alongside root credentials
Integrating Root Lockdown into Account Vending
In organizations that create many AWS accounts (via Control Tower, AFT, or custom automation), root lockdown should be built into the account creation pipeline, not applied manually after the fact.
AWS Control Tower Landing Zone automatically:
Enables CloudTrail in all enrolled accounts
Enforces Security Hub CIS Benchmark checks including root MFA
Applies SCPs that restrict root usage in member accounts
Emails the root account owner with initial login instructions
If you use a custom account vending process (e.g., a Lambda-backed Service Catalog product or Terraform), add a post-vend step that:

# Example: post-account-creation Lambda that verifies root lockdown baseline
# Called after account creation by the vending pipeline

import boto3

def verify_root_lockdown(account_id, audit_role_arn):
    sts = boto3.client('sts')
    assumed = sts.assume_role(
        RoleArn=audit_role_arn,
        RoleSessionName='RootLockdownVerify'
    )
    creds = assumed['Credentials']
    
    iam = boto3.client('iam',
        aws_access_key_id=creds['AccessKeyId'],
        aws_secret_access_key=creds['SecretAccessKey'],
        aws_session_token=creds['SessionToken']
    )
    
    summary = iam.get_account_summary()['SummaryMap']
    
    issues = []
    if summary.get('AccountAccessKeysPresent', 0) > 0:
        issues.append('Root access keys exist — delete immediately')
    if summary.get('AccountMFAEnabled', 0) == 0:
        issues.append('MFA not enabled on root user')
    
    if issues:
        # Trigger a remediation workflow or alert
        sns = boto3.client('sns')
        sns.publish(
            TopicArn='arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:MGMT_ACCOUNT:security-alerts',
            Subject=f'Root lockdown incomplete: {account_id}',
            Message=f'Account {account_id} has root security gaps:\n' + '\n'.join(issues)
        )
        return {'compliant': False, 'issues': issues}
    
    return {'compliant': True, 'issues': []}

Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) supports customization templates that run immediately after account creation. Root lockdown verification is a natural fit for the aft-account-customizations repository alongside other baseline configurations.
Summary

Locking away root credentials follows a specific sequence: delete all root access keys, enable hardware MFA, set a strong random password, store everything in a secrets vault with a documented runbook, and configure CloudTrail alerts that fire on any root login. For accounts inside an AWS Organization, apply an SCP that denies all root actions in member accounts as a final defense-in-depth measure.

The measure of success is simple: root credentials should be harder to access than the most sensitive data in your system, and every use of root should generate an alert and a documented record. If root is used without an audit trail, your lockdown is incomplete.
